coredata.dc:description: Transition metal layered double hydroxides have extremely high specific capacitances but suffer from poor rate performance and cycling stability due to their low conductivity and structural stability. In this study, cobalt-coordinated carbon quantum dots (CoCQDs) were designed and synthesized to enhance the energy storage performance of nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDH). Nickel and cobalt ions were co-electrodeposited with the CoCQDs to form a NiCo-LDH based composite electrode (denoted as CoC@LDH). Since the CoCQDs participated in the formation of the NiCo-LDH, the carbon quantum dots could be strongly bonded to the NiCo-LDH nanosheets through coordination interactions. Thus, the conductivity as well as the structure stability of the NiCo-LDH was effectively improved, which greatly boosted the cycle stability and rate performance of the NiCo-LDH. Several CoCQDs with different Co contents (nCoCQDs, n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) were fabricated and their effects on the performance of the resultant electrodes nCoC@LDH were investigated. The 1.0CoC@LDH electrode exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 1867 F g–1 at 1 A-g–1, along with a significantly enhanced capacitance retention of 84.6 % after 6000 cycles at 5 A g–1 (benchmark 49.5 %). This ingenious design provides a new avenue for fabricating pseudo-capacitive materials with unprecedented high performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal layered double hydroxides have extremely high specific capacitances but suffer from poor rate performance and cycling stability due to their low conductivity and structural stability. In this study, cobalt-coordinated carbon quantum dots (CoCQDs) were designed and synthesized to enhance the energy storage performance of nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDH). Nickel and cobalt ions were co-electrodeposited with the CoCQDs to form a NiCo-LDH based composite electrode (denoted as CoC@LDH). Since the CoCQDs participated in the formation of the NiCo-LDH, the carbon quantum dots could be strongly bonded to the NiCo-LDH nanosheets through coordination interactions. Thus, the conductivity as well as the structure stability of the NiCo-LDH was effectively improved, which greatly boosted the cycle stability and rate performance of the NiCo-LDH. Several CoCQDs with different Co contents (nCoCQDs, n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) were fabricated and their effects on the performance of the resultant electrodes nCoC@LDH were investigated. The 1.0CoC@LDH electrode exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 1867 F g–1 at 1 A-g–1, along with a significantly enhanced capacitance retention of 84.6 % after 6000 cycles at 5 A g–1 (benchmark 49.5 %). This ingenious design provides a new avenue for fabricating pseudo-capacitive materials with unprecedented high performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion rechargeable batteries are used to provide clean energy for vehicles, but they have problems of low power density, long charging time, as well as some safety issues, e.g., inflammable and explosive [1,2]. Supercapacitors as ideal supplements for the lithium-ion batteries, are getting growing attention for their ultrafast charge/discharge rates and long service lives. However, the energy density of supercapacitors is still considerably lower than that of lithium batteries, which impedes the extension of their applications. Pseudocapacitors, featured with fast and reversible faradaic charge-transfer reactions, may bridge the energy density gap between electric double layer supercapacitors and lithium-ion batteries [3–5]. Among them, nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCo-LDH) can deliver a theoretical specific capacitance as high as 4100 F g–1, which would be a promising candidate for addressing the challenge of energy density [6–8]. However, NiCo-LDH suffers from poor cycle stability and rate performance due to its low electrical conductivity and structural stability [9,10].

To address the weaknesses of NiCo-LDH, doping with heteroatoms (Al, Zn, Mg, S) [11–14] or incorporating with conductive compounds (CNTs, MOF, Ti3C2Tx) has been widely attempted [15–17]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are of special attention, due to their nanosize effects, edge effects, and abundant surface functional groups [18–21]. It has been reported that NiCo-LDH can be coupled with high-fermi-level graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through electrostatic interactions [22]. The Fermi level gap between the two components triggers a charge separation at interfaces which enhances charge transport in the composite. PN heterojunctions between nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and NiCo-LDH can also improve the conductivity [23]. Recently, it has been revealed that CQDs can increase the structure stability of NiCo-LDH nanoarrays and effectively prevent the collapse of NiCo-LDH induced from volume changes over a prolonged period of cycling [24], as a result of the electrostatic interaction between CQDs and metal ions. The current strategies of combining CQDs and NiCo-LDH are mainly via weak interactions, e.g., electrostatic absorption and van der Waals force, which have a limited ability to modify the friable NiCo-LDH by using CQDs.

In this study, we combined CQDs with NiCo-LDH through a strong chemical conjugation — coordination bonding. CQDs coordinated with an increasing content of cobalt (nCoCQD, n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) were prepared firstly, and then co-electrodeposited with nickel and cobalt ions to deposit nCoCQD on NiCo-LDH nanosheets. The obtained nCoCQD/NiCo-LDH composites were denoted as nCoC@LDH, n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0. Cobalt ions in the CQDs were also used as a Co source to participate in the formation of NiCo-LDH and thus, the CQDs could be bound to the NiCo-LDH through coordination bonds. The conductivity as well as the structure stability of the NiCo-LDH was effectively enhanced. As a result, the 1.0CoC@LDH composite electrode delivered a high specific capacitance of 1867 F g–1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and an excellent rate performance (89.88 % retention). Meanwhile, the composite electrode exhibited good cycle performance, with a high capacitance retention of 84.6 % after 6000 cycles at 5 A g−1. Moreover, an asymmetric supercapacitor was fabricated by using the CoC@LDH composite as cathode and activated carbon (AC) as anode and the supercapacitor delivered high charge storage capabilities of 44.67 W h kg−1 at 750 W kg−1 and 38.25 W h kg−1 at 7500 W kg−1.

Nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O, 99 %), Cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co(CH3COO)2⋅4H2O, 99 %), pyrrole and potassium hydroxide (KOH, 95 %) were purchased from Aladdin. Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2⋅6H2O, 99 %), p-phenylenediamine (PPDA, 98 %), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA, 99.5 %), were supplied by Macklin. Carbon cloth and activated carbon (AC) were provided by Taiwan Carbon Energy and Fuzhou Yihuan Carbon, respectively.

1.0CoCQDs precursor was prepared by dissolving 0.3568 g of CoCl2·6H2O, 0.8768 g of EDTA, and 0.84 g of PPDA in 60 mL of deionized (DI) water under stirring at 60 °C. The precursor solution was sealed in a stainless steel hydrothermal reactor. After a reaction at 180 °C for 6 hous, the obtained solution was purified by dialysis (Mw 500) in DI water for 48 h. 1.0CoCQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying. The preparation methods of 0.5CoCQDs and 2.0CoCQDs are similar, but the amounts of CoCl2·6H2O were 0.5 times and 2 times that of 1.0CoCQDs, respectively.

nCoC@LDH were fabricated by simple one-step electrodeposition. The carbon cloth used for the electrodeposition was activated first [25]. Then, 10 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 10 mmol of Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O and 0.04 g of nCQDs were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. Electrodeposition was performed in a three-electrode system by galvanostatic deposition. The activated carbon cloth was used as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference, and Pt foil as a counter electrode. After five cycles of energized and left electrodeposition, nCoC@LDH (n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) were obtained on the activated carbon cloth. The electrodeposition was energized with a negative current of 4 mA for 120 s, then shelved for 30 s. The reason for the 30 s holding time is to ensure that the concentration of the solution around the deposited sample does not fluctuate significantly and that the deposition is uniform [26]. On the carbon cloth, the load of each electrode material were 1.12 mg (0.5CoC@LDH), 1.24 mg (1.0CoC@LDH), 1.19 mg (2.0CoC@LDH), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of reference NiCo-LDH by electrodeposition
ce:section.ce:para: By using electrodeposition, a reference NiCo-LDH sample was also made on activated carbon cloth. Initially, 100 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 10 mmol of Co(CH3COO)2⋅4H2O and 10 mmol of Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O. The electrodeposition was performed using a 4 mA negative current for 120 s and held for 30 s each of 5 cycles. On the carbon cloth, the load of NiCo-LDH was 1.05 mg.

Electron microscopies (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800; TEM, Talos F200X G2) were used to study the morphologies of the samples. Crystallographic information was obtained by X-ray diffractions (XRD, D8 ADVANCE with Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected on an AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) measurements were executed on a Nicolet 6700.

The electrochemical properties of the nCoC@LDH electrodes were measured either in a three-electrode or two-electrode measurement. In the three-electrode measurements, the nCoC@LDH electrodes were used as the working electrodes, a Pt plate as the counter electrode, and Hg/HgO as the reference electrode. In the two-electrode measurements, activated carbon (AC) was used as the anode, and nCoC@LDH as the cathode. The activated carbon electrode was prepared by a traditional slurry coating method [27] and had a mass loading of 4.16 mg. On a biological VMP-3, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the electrodes and the asymmetric supercapacitor (ACS) were carried out. A Neware CT-4000 multi-channel battery tester was used to examine the cycle and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) performance.

The specific capacitance (Cs , F g–1) of the electrodes could be calculated from their GCD curves according to the equation [28]:
C s

m Δ V
where I (A) is the charge–discharge current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, m (g) is the mass of the electrode and ΔV (V) is the potential window.

A hybrid supercapacitor was made using 1.0CoC@LDH as the cathode, AC as the anode and 6 M KOH as the electrolyte. Active carbon, acetylene black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) were mixed in a mass ratio of 8:1:1 and then dissolved in N-methylpyrrolidone. The obtained slurry was pasted on the activated carbon cloth to finally obtain the AC electrode.

The synthesis process of CoCQDs is schematically described in Fig. 1 a. The aqueous solution contains well dispersed CoCQDs which shows light brown color. UV–vis spectra in Fig. 1b demonstrate that the CQDs solution exhibits a strong absorption at 230 nm, which is caused by the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC structure [31,32]. Additionally, there is an absorption peak at 520 nm which might be attributed to n-π* transition of surface functional groups of carbon dots [33]. It is worth noting that the absorption peak at 520 nm diminishes as the Co content rises. This is caused by Co2+ coordination with the functional groups on carbon dots [34]. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of the nCoCQDs (Fig. 1c) show a new absorption peak at around 600 cm−1 and higher the Co content, i.e., the larger the n, the stronger the absorption. This absorption peak can be attributed to the stretching vibration of Co-O [35–37], which provides an evidence of strong coordination interactions between Co2+ and CQDs.

The surface chemistry of 1.0CoCQDs was further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). A full XPS spectrum of 1.0CoCQDs (Fig. S1a) shows peaks of Co 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, but the Co 1 s peak is missing in a XPS spectrum of CQDs (Fig. S1d). Fig. 1d shows a high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of 1.0CoCQDs which can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 399.3, 399.8, 400.9, and 401.9 eV, respectively. They are corresponding to pyridinic N, Co-N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N [38], demonstrating that Co2+ coordinates with nitrogen. Fig. S1a shows a C 1 s spectrum that can be deconvoluted into five peaks for CC, CC, CN, N CO, and CO at 284.6, 285.5.1, 286.4, 288.3, and 289.1 eV [39,40], respectively. A Co 2p spectrum given in Fig. S1b shows two peaks, one at 780.7 and another 796.2 eV and they can be assigned to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively [41], confirming the presence of Co in the CoCQDs. High-resolution N 1 s and C 1 s spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. S1e and S1f respectively. From the above analysis, it can be concluded that Co-coordinated carbon dots were successfully prepared.

Due to the positive charger character, CoCQDs can be deposited under negative potentials like Ni2+ and Co2+ (Figure S2), so that they can be incorporated with NiCo-LDH by electrodeposition under negative potentials [42,43]. Fig. 2 a describes the preparation process of the CoC@LDH nanosheets. In the process, Ni2+, Co2+, and CoCQDs in the solution migrated to the carbon cloth side under a 4 mA negative current. After 5 cycles, Ni2+ and Co2+ in the solution as well as Co2+ in the CQDs served as nickel and cobalt sources to form NiCo-LDH. At the same time, CoCQDs were incorporated into the NiCo-LDH nanosheets (Fig. 2b). The connections between the CoCQDs and NiCo-LDH through hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and chemical bonds are depicted in an enlarged schematic drawing shown in Fig. 2b. The morphologies of the NiCo-LDH and nCoC@LDH were examined by SEM and the results are presented in Fig. 2c–f. The results show nanosheet-like CoC@LDH anchored on the carbon cloth substrate. The CoC@LDH nanosheets are interconnected, forming a network that is beneficial for ion adsorption/desorption [44,45]. However, compared to the pure NiCo-LDH, the CoC@LDH nanosheets are obviously sparser which would provide more buffer spaces for volume expansion during a charge/discharge process [23,46,47].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reveals a nanosheet structure of 1.0CoC@LDH (Fig. 3 a). The creation of nickel–cobalt hydroxide is successfully verified by distinct polycrystalline diffraction rings (Figure S3), and these diffraction rings can be indexed as crystal planes (012), (018) and (113) of NiCo-LDH [22]. Furthermore, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of 1.0CoC@LDH (Fig. 3c, d) show the lattice spacings of 0.253, 0.207 and 0.212 nm. The 0.253 and 0.207 nm match the d-spacings of (012) and (107) planes of NiCo-LDH [48–50], respectively and the 0.212 nm lattice fringes can be indexed to the (100) plane of CoCQDs [23], suggesting that carbon dots embedded in the NiCo-LDH. In addition, EDS/TEM elemental mappings show the distribution of Ni, Co, and O elements in the NiCo-LDH (Fig. 3b, e–g). The circled areas in Fig. 2b show low concentrations of Co and Ni, so the areas might be carbon quantum dots with a size of ∼5 nm, demonstrating a good dispersion of CoCQD in the NiCo-LDH. This is also a favorable outcome of the co-electrodeposition process.

The chemical compositions of the nCoC@LDH series were further characterized by FTIR and the results are given in Fig. 4 a and 4d. As shown in Fig. 4a, the peaks at 1570 and 1386 cm−1 are assigned to CO and C-OH groups of CoCQDs [51,52], respectively. With the increase of the Co content, a red shift of the CO peak is observed. This red shift is due to the coordination of Co2+ in the carbon quantum dots with O in the CO bond [34]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 526 and 630 cm−1 are assigned to the M−O−M (M: Co2+, Ni2+) stretching vibrations in the nCoC@LDH series [22,53]. The FTIR results indicate successful coupling of CoCQDs and NiCo-LDH in the nCoC@LDH composites. XRD patterns of nCoC@LDH (Fig. 4b) show typical patterns of hydrotalcite-like layered double hydroxides. Peaks at 2θ = 12.4°, 34.9°, and 61.2°, can be indexed as (003), (012), and (110) planes, respectively [54,55]. Two broad peaks at 26.7° and 44.5° are attributed to the diffraction of the carbon cloth.

To demonstrate that the CoCQDs as one of the Co sources participated in the formation of the NiCo-LDH, we directly electrodeposited 1.0CoCQDs on carbon cloth without the presence of any other Ni2+ and Co2+ sources. Fig. 4c shows that CoCQDs were deposited onto the carbon cloth and formed nanosheets. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 4d also confirms that CoCQDs is one of the Co sources in the synthesis of the NiCo-LDH. This is evident from the appearance of a new absorption vibration peak at 500–600 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of Co–O. Therefore, carbon quantum dots can be chemically bonded to NiCo-LDH nanosheets.

XPS was used to determine the chemical composition and structure of the composite electrode materials. Full spectra shown in Fig. 4e confirm the presence of Ni, Co, O, and C in the composite electrodes. N 1 s peak is visible on the spectrum of 1.0CoC@LDH, which originates from CoCQDs. Observably, high-resolution spectra of Ni 2p and Co 2p show two spin–orbit doublets and two shaken satellites (labeled as Sat.) [56,57], as depicted in Fig. 4f, g. The two peaks at 855.2 and 872.9 eV of the Ni 2p spectrum correspond to Ni2+, and the two peaks at 856.5 and 874.8 eV are related to Ni3+. Similarly, 780.3 and 795.8 eV of the Co spectrum correspond to Co3+, while 781. 7 and 797.3 eV relate to Co2+. In terms of the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4h), peaks at 398.8, 400.1, 402.9, and 406.0 eV correspond to pyridinic N, Co–N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. In addition, Fig. 4i shows the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of O 1 s, 531.1 eV can be attributed to the M−O bond, and 532.7 eV can be attributed to the OH bond.

The electrochemical properties of nCoC@LDH were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests and the results are given in Fig. 5 a and b. The redox pair of the CV curves (Fig. 5a) and the presence of two distinct potential plateaus in the GCD curves (Fig. 5b) demonstrate their battery-type energy storage behavior. The CV at 10 mV s−1 shows that 1.0CoC@LDH exhibits the largest integrated area and the best capacitive performance. This is further confirmed by the GCD curves obtained at 1 A g–1 that 1.0CoC@LDH has the longest discharge time and therefore the largest specific capacitance. The capacitances of these composites are calculated from the GCD curves. At 1 A g–1, the specific capacitances of NiCo-LDH, 0.5CoC@LDH, 1.0CoC@LDH and 2.0CoC@LDH are 1451, 1793, 1867 and 1649 F g–1, respectively. This result indicates that CoCQDs can effectively increase the capacitance of NiCo-LDH. The SEM images (Fig. 2c–f) show that the CoC@LDH nanosheets are more loosely distributed and less neatly stacked. Because the nCoC@LDH nanoarrays vertically arrange on the surface of the carbon fiber, the obtained electrode displays a well-defined nanostructure with abundant active sites and convenient ion diffusion paths. This is favorable for electrolyte to entry into the entire nanoarrays [58,59]. Figure S4 shows CV curves of nCoC@LDH at various scan rates and GCD curves at different current densities. The shape of the curves was preserved even at high scan rates, indicating the excellent rate survivability of CoC@LDH. Meanwhile, no obvious shape change was observed in the GCD curve profiles of all the samples under different current densities, suggesting good reversibility and rate capability of the CoC@LDH electrode. To check the contribution of Co in CoCQDs to the capacitance, we electrodeposited 1.0CoCQDs directly on carbon cloth without additional Ni2+ and Co2+ sources (the same electrodeposition process as 1.0CoC@LDH). Then we collected the CV of 1.0CoCQDs at 10 mV s−1 (Fig. S5a). Compared to 1.0CoC@LDH, the capacitance from the CV of 1.0CoCQDs is significantly lower. In addition, there is no significant redox peaks in the CV curve of 1.0CoCQDs. Therefore, the contribution of Co in CoCQDs to the capacitance is very weak.

From the GCD curves shown in Fig. 5b, the relationship between specific capacitance and current density was obtained and it was shown in Fig. 5c. Compared to the NiCo-LDH the capacitance retentions of nCoC@LDH are significantly better at 20 A g–1 (Fig. S5b), 78.4 % retention for NiCo-LDH, 86.2 % retention for 0.5CoC@LDH, 89.9 % retention for 1.0CoC@LDH and 84.5 % retention for 2.0CoC@LDH. It is obvious that doping CoCQDs into NiCo-LDH can yield remarkable rate performance. EIS can be used to evaluate the kinetic behavior of the as-achieved electrodes. Nyquist plots of NiCo-LDH and nCoC@LDH are shown in Fig. 5d. Zview software was used to build the equivalent circuit to fit the obtained EIS data. From the equivalent circuit, Rs is the sum of the solution resistance, intrinsic resistance, and interface resistance. Rct stands for the charge-transfer resistance associated with Faradaic reactions. W1 stands for ion diffusion in the electrolyte. And CPE1 is the constant phase element [60]. Fig. 5d shows that the Rs values of the NiCo-LDH and nCoC@LDH (n = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) electrodes are 0.993, 0.695, 0.687, 0.803 Ω, respectively. While the Rct values are 1.318, 0.904, 0.733, 0.983 Ω, respectively. The Rct and Rs of the 1.0CoC@LDH electrode are the lowest, indicating that the introduction of 1.0CoCQDs can effectively reduce the transfer resistance. This reduction accelerates the electron exchange rate of the surface Faradaic reaction and improves the electrochemical performance of the electrode. Moreover, the rate performance of 1.0CoC@LDH is the best (89.9 %) at a high current density of 20 A g–1. This is due to the smaller interfacial transfer resistance of 1.0CoC@LDH, and the easier redox electron transfer. The electronic band structure can be adjusted by carbon quantum dots doping, thereby maintaining faster electron/ion migration and promoting interfacial redox reactions [22]. Besides, the hierarchical nanoplate network structure of the electrodes could provide abundant channels for ion diffusion and transformation.

The charge storage mechanism of the nCoC@LDH electrodes was further investigated by analyzing their CV curves. The relationship between diffusion and capacitive control processes is given by [61]:
i = a

where i is the peak current of the electrode, and v is the scan rate. The value of the variable b can be calculated from the slope of a plot of log(i) against log(v). When b = 1, the charge storage is capacitance dominated. And when b = 0.5, the electrochemical reaction is controlled by diffusion. The calculated b values of anodic and cathodic peak currents are 0.82 and 0.89, 0.78 and 0.69, 0.74 and 0.65, and 0.78 and 0.67 for the NiCo-LDH, 0.5CoC@LDH, 1.0CoC@LDH and 2.0CoC@LDH electrodes, respectively (Fig. 5e). nCoC@LDH have higher b values than the pure NiCo-LDH, thus the CoCQDs promote the capacitive behavior of the NiCo-LDH. The contributions of the diffusion-control and capacitance-control to the electrochemical process were further calibrated in accordance with the modified power-law, as illustrated in Fig. 5f. As the scanning rate increases from 1 mV s−1 to 20 mV s−1, the diffusion-controlled contribution fractions of the nCoC@LDH electrodesare larger than that of the pure NiCo-LDH electrode, indicating that the strong coupling between CoCQDs and NiCo-LDH in the nCoC@LDH electrodes. The fractions of the pseudocapacitance of 1.0CoC@LDH increase from 75.88 % to 92.45 % when the scan rate rises from 1 mV s−1 to 20 mV s−1, indicating that the reaction kinetics is mainly controlled by surface capacitive processes. In general, fast ion transfer is not achievable with diffusion-controlled behavior to support fast electrochemical reactions at high scan rates. Fast ion migration and effective redox reaction are made possible by doping CoCQDs into NiCo-LDH due to the capacitive behavior that compensates for quick electro-kinetics. Furthermore, the hierarchical nanoplate network structure of the nCoC@LDH electrode can offer abundant pathways for ion diffusion and transformation, resulting in the excellent rate performance of 1.0CoC@LDH at high scan rate.

More importantly, the 1.0CoC@LDH electrode showed excellent cycling stability with 84.6 % retention after 6000 cycles at 5 A g–1, and high coulombic efficiency of over 95 % (Fig. 5g). As can be seen from Fig. 5d, the cycle performance of nCoC@LDH was significantly improved. The decrease in cycle performance of 2.0CoC@LDH might be caused by excessive carbon dots doping. The 1.0CoC@LDH has a capacitance retention of 84.6 %, which is much higher than the pure NiCo-LDH's retention of 49.5 %. The morphologies of 1.0CoC@LDH and the pure NiCo-LDH after a long period of cycling were examined by SEM and the results are shown in Figure S6. The pure NiCo-LDH suffered a serious deformation and peeled off the carbon cloth after only 6000 cycles. The results are consistent with the poor intrinsic cycling structural stability of NiCo-LDH. Ni2+ and Co2+ underwent a continuous redox reaction at the active sites of the pure NiCo-LDH in the charge–discharge cycles, and the valence shifts in them may alter the stress and structure of NiCo-LDHs’ host layers as a result of the Jahn-Teller effect [62,63]. In comparison, as shown in Fig. S6a and S6b, 1.0CoC@LDH was neither exfoliated nor deformed after long charge–discharge cycles up to 6000. Besides, TEM images and FTIR spectra of 1.0CoC@LDH after 6000 cycles are shown in Figure S7. The lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.218 nm (Figure S7c) are attributed to (100) of the CoCQDs, indicating the presence of CoCQDs in 1.0CoC@LDH after the cycling. The lattice spacings of 0.206 and 0.251 nm are assigned to the (107) and (012) planes of the NiCo-LDH, further demonstrating that the structure of NiCo-LDH was maintained after the cycling. In addition, the FTIR spectra of the CoCQDs and 1.0CoC@LDH after cycling are compared in Figure S7f. The peaks at 1570 and 1386 cm−1 are assigned to CO and C-OH groups of the CoCQDs, respectively. This suggests that the CoCQDs were preserved after the cycling. The exceptional cycle stability of the 1.0CoC@LDH electrode can be attributed to the following factors:1) theloose arrangement and wrinkled morphology can accommodate more volume expansions and shrinks of the NiCo-LDH matrix during the charging/discharging; 2) CoCQDs are a tough material and they can serve as bridges via strong chemical bonds and prevent structural collapse [64]. By comparing 1.0CoC@LDH to other NiCo-LDH electrode materials (Table 1 ), we may gain a deeper understanding of the remarkable electrochemical performance. The specific capacitance of 1.0CoC@LDH is better than most of the reported NiCo-LDH materials. In particular, due to the fast electron/ion migration and elevated interfacial redox reactions induced by CoCQDs, 1.0CoC@LDH exhibited excellent rate performance that surpasses other reported works. The excellent cycle stability and structural integrity of 1.0CoC@LDH are probably due to the combined effects of toughening (caused by CoCQDs) and a loose structure.

In order to explore potential applications of 1.0CoC@LDH, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was assembled using 1.0CoC@LDH as the cathode, AC as the anode and 6 M KOH as the electrolyte. The CVs of the two electrodes are shown in Fig. 6 a. The potential range of the AC electrode is −1.0 to 0 V, while the potential range of 1.0CoC@LDH is 0 to 0.5 V. Therefore, the ASC has a potential window range from 0 to 1.5 V. The CV curves of the ASC with scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 6b. No significant change in terms of the shapes of the CV curves can be seen when the scan rate was increased from 10 to 100 mV s−1, indicating the asymmetric supercapacitor has good capacitive performance. Fig. 6c displays GCD curves of the ASC at various current densities (voltage window 0–1.5 V). The GCD curves have an almost symmetrical shape, which suggests a high level of electrochemical reversibility. The capacitances of the ASC at the current densities ranging from 1 to 10 A g–1 were 142.94, 138.71, 135.15, 130.05, 126.13, and 122.41 F g–1, respectively (Fig. 6d). The energy density and power density of the ASC can be calculated from the GCD curves. From Ragone plots (Fig. 6e), the ASC exhibited a high energy density of 44.67 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 750 W kg−1 and still maintained 38.25 Wh kg−1 at 7500 W kg−1. Compared with other studies, this ASC device exhibited higher energy and power density [7,14,17,22,41,72–76]. Impressively, the capacitance retention of our ASC was 83.6 % after 5000 cycles at 2 A g–1 (Fig. 6f). Moreover, the ASC maintained an ultrahigh Coulombic efficiency of > 98 % for the duration of the lengthy cycle, which is crucial for long-term cycling stability. Therefore, the introduction of the CoCQDs maintained the structural stability of 1.0CoC@LDH and contributed to the excellent cycling stability of the ASC. The experimental results show that the 1.0CoC@LDH electrode is one of the most promising candidates in the field of practical energy storage.

In this paper, NiCo-LDH nanosheets modified by cobalt coordinated CQDs were successfully prepared by electrodeposition. The coordinated connection between CQDs and NiCo-LDH made a great contribution to the enhanced electrical conductivity of the obtained nCoC@LDH. When used as an electrode in a supercapacitor, a high specific capacitance of 1867 F g–1 and excellent rate performance (89.88 % retention at 20 A g–1) were obtained. More importantly, the resultant nCoC@LDH electrodes exhibited a loose nanosheets arrangement, which could provide spaces for volume expansion during the charging and discharging cycles. Meanwhile, CoCQDs served as a toughening material to bridge the broken NiCo-LDH nanosheets by chemical bonds. Among the composite electrodes, 1.0CoC@LDH presented the best electrochemical stability (84.6 % capacitance retention after 6000 cycles at 5 A g–1). Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor (1.0CoC@LDH//AC) achieved high energy densities of 44.67 W h kg−1 (power densities of 750 W kg−1) and 38.25 W h kg−1 (7500 W kg−1). This study may provide a strategy to construct a coordination connection between CQDs and NiCo-LDH, which offers a practical approach to enhance the electrochemical performance of NiCo-LDHs materials.
